title: Ben Beecham's Github
text:
Victor Wembanyama’s expectations coming into the league were astronomical. After thousands of fans witnessed the 7-foot-4 unicorn handle the ball and shoot 30-foot 3-pointers on social media, many expected him to instantly be a superstar.
Wembanyama’s first two years in the league have been solid. He was named to his first All-Star team this season and he has to ability to put up absurd statlines. However, his season was cut short due to blood clots this year and the Spurs have failed to even make the play-in during his early career. Wembanyama has been an All-Star, but has he met the generational expectations he had?

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(ggrepel)

blockspergame <- read_csv("blockspergame.csv")|>
  select(Player, Season, `BLK/G`, MP)

trueshooting <- read_csv("trueshooting.csv")

wemby_shooting <- trueshooting |>
  filter(Player == "Victor Wembanyama")

rebounding <- read_csv("rebounding.csv")

wemby_rebounding <- rebounding |>
  filter(Player == "Victor Wembanyama")

ggplot()+
  geom_point(
    data=trueshooting, aes(x=`FGA...4`, y=`TS%...3`), alpha=.25
  )+
  geom_point(
    data = wemby_shooting,  
    aes(x = `FGA...4`, y = `TS%...3`), colour="black", size=4
  )+
  geom_text_repel(
    data = wemby_shooting, aes(x = `FGA...4`, y = `TS%...3`, label = "Victor Wembanyama"), nudge_y =.018
  )+
  labs(
    title = "Victor Wembanyama is a subpar shooter",
    subtitle = "The former first-overall pick has had a middling true shooting percentage in both seasons of his career so far. He\ndid improve his percentage this season, however the sample size is smaller due to him only playing 46 games.",
    x = "Field Goal Attempts",
    y = "True Shooting Percentage",
    caption = "Source: Stathead Basketball | Ben Beecham"
  )+theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 








Wembanyama’s game leaves a bit to be desired on the offensive end. He has spent a lot of time around the perimeter for someone of his stature and has not converted it at a high rate. Although he hasn’t used his length to his advantage as much on offense, he sure has on defense. Many had concerns about Wembanyama’s weight coming into the league and thought he would have to bulk up if he wanted to survive in the paint in the NBA. That isn’t the case.

blockspergame |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    `BLK/G` = "Blocks per Game",
    Player = "Player",
    MP = "Minutes"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Victor Wembanyama is already a generational defender",
    subtitle = "The Frenchman's first two seasons are both top 10 in blocks per game since 2000."
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("Source: Stathead Basketball | Ben Beecham")
  ) |> 
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(4)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=15)
     )
  ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "black"),
      cell_text(color = "#c4ced4")
    ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = Player == "Victor Wembanyama")
  )







Victor Wembanyama is already a generational defender


The Frenchman's first two seasons are both top 10 in blocks per game since 2000.


Player
Season
Blocks per Game
Minutes




Victor Wembanyama
2024-25
3.8
1527


Theo Ratliff
2000-01
3.7
1800


Hassan Whiteside
2015-16
3.7
2125


Serge Ibaka
2011-12
3.7
1792


Theo Ratliff
2003-04
3.6
2664


Marcus Camby
2007-08
3.6
2758


Victor Wembanyama
2023-24
3.6
2106


Ben Wallace
2001-02
3.5
2921


Myles Turner
2020-21
3.4
1455


Andrei Kirilenko
2004-05
3.3
1349



Source: Stathead Basketball | Ben Beecham








Wembanyama is also, not surprisingly, a great rebounder. His rebounding was another concern coming into the league due to his weight. He has also silenced the doubters there.

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=rebounding, aes(x=reorder(Player, `TRB/48`), y=`TRB/48`), 
          stat="identity", alpha=0.2) + 
  geom_bar(data=wemby_rebounding, aes(x=reorder(Player, `TRB/48`), y=`TRB/48`), 
          stat="identity", fill="black") +
  coord_flip()+
  labs(
    title = "Wembanyama is one of the best young rebounders",
    subtitle = "The Spur has been to make good use of his absurd eight-foot wingspan on the boards. He ranks seventh among\nfirst and second year players since 1990 in rebounds per 48 minutes, putting him right behind Shaq.",
    x = "",
    y = "Rebounds Per 48 Minutes",
    caption = "Source: Stathead Basketball | Ben Beecham"
  )+theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )








Before coming into the league, Wembanyama’s expectations were set so high that the only prospect many analysts compared him to was LeBron James. Although his offensive game is still far from The King’s, Wembanyama has separated himself as a one-of-a-kind combination of defensive prowess and offensive versatility. However, if he truly needs to meet his expectations, he needs to become more efficient on offense.

title: Ben Beecham's Github
text:
The Washington Commanders’ turnaround from last season was incredible. After being the second-worst team in the NFL, Washington went and made it one game shy of the Super Bowl. The Commanders made a lot of moves to prompt that turnaround, however, the most important one was drafting quarterback Jayden Daniels.
Daniels put together one of the most impressive rookie campaigns the league has ever seen. He lit up the league throw the air and broke the rookie quarterback rushing yard record. Daniels’ season was one for the ages; however, was it the best rookie quarterback season ever?
First, look at the passing stats. This bubble chart splits every rookie quarterback since 1975 who started at least eight games and won at least four games into four quadrants using completion percentage and adjusted net yards per attempt. Adjusted net yards per attempt considers stats such as interceptions and sacks making it one of the most comprehensive passing stats.
Because the data works with 50 years of data, the stats are put on indexes. These indexes adjust statistics by taking in historical league averages to make it easier to compare across different eras. On the indexes, 100 is the league average. The bubble size represents how many games the player won.

library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggalt)

rookiequarterbacks <- read_csv("advanced_stats_with_wins.csv")

qb_win_diff <- read_csv("qb_win_differential.csv")

qbrushing <- read_csv("qbrushing.csv")

jd <- rookiequarterbacks |>
  filter(Player == "Jayden Daniels")

jd_rush <- qbrushing |>
  filter(Player == "Jayden Daniels")

dak <- rookiequarterbacks |>
  filter(Player == "Dak Prescott")

dakrush <- qbrushing |>
  filter(Player == "Dak Prescott")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data = rookiequarterbacks,  aes(x = `ANY/A+`, y = `Cmp%+`, size = W...5),alpha = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = jd, aes(x = `ANY/A+`, y = `Cmp%+`, size = W...5), colour="#800"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = dak,  aes(x = `ANY/A+`, y = `Cmp%+`, size = W...5), colour="#003594"
  )+
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 100) +
  geom_text(aes(x=83.2, y=83, label="Inefficient Passers, Low Cmp%"), color="darkblue")+
  geom_text(aes(x=117, y=110, label="Efficient Passers, High Cmp%"), color="darkblue")+
  geom_text(aes(x=107.5, y=122.4, label="Jayden Daniels"), color="black")+
  geom_text(aes(x=125.5, y=126.4, label = "Dak Prescott"), color="black")+
  labs(
    title = "Daniels had one of the best rookie arms in history",
    subtitle = "Jayden Daniels was one of the most elite first-year passers ever, but... he isn't the single best.",
    x = "Adjusted Net Yards per Attempt Index",
    y = "Completion Percentage Index",
    caption="Source: Stathead Football | Ben Beecham",
    size= "Wins",
  ) +
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )+
  scale_size(range = c(1, 13), name="Wins")








Jayden Daniels’ numbers put him in a small group of elite rookie passers. His throws were efficient and he completed them at a high rate, except Dak Prescott was better at both of those things. His passes were marginally more efficient and he completed his passes at a slightly higher rate. However, Daniels’ skill set can’t be discussed without discussing his rushing ability. This scatterplot measures yards per carry and total attempts. There is one dot away from the rest.

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = qbrushing, aes(x = `Att...37`, y = `Y/A...3`),
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = jd_rush,  
    aes(x = `Att...37`, y = `Y/A...3`), colour="#800", size=7.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = dakrush,  aes(x = `Att...37`, y = `Y/A...3`), colour="#003594", size=7.5
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = jd_rush, aes(x = `Att...37`, y = `Y/A...3`, label = "Jayden Daniels"),nudge_y = 1
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = dakrush, aes(x = `Att...37`, y = `Y/A...3`, label = "Dak Prescott"),nudge_y = 1
  )+
  geom_text(aes(x=35, y=1, label="Low Volume, Inefficient Rushers"), color="darkblue")+
  geom_text(aes(x=115, y=6.5, label="High Volume, Efficient Rushers"), color="darkblue")+
  geom_hline(yintercept = 4)+
  geom_vline(xintercept = 75)+
  labs(
    title = "Daniels is the best rookie quarterback rusher ever",
    subtitle = "Jayden Daniels averaged about six yards per carry while running the ball a lot more than any other quarterback",
    x = "Rushing Attempts",
    y = "Yards per Attempt",
    caption = "Source: Stathead Football | Ben Beecham"
  )+theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 








Daniels is far and away the best rookie rusher of all time. Although he was slightly less efficient than a few quarterbacks, he had way more carries than those players. He was also a much better dual-threat player than Dak.
It’s also important to discuss how big Daniels’ impact was to winning. Rookie quarterbacks are expected to turn around a team’s fortune and he did just that for the Commanders. This dumbbell chart shows how many games a team won with their rookie quarterback compared to the season before they drafted them.

ggplot() + 
  geom_dumbbell(
    data=qb_win_diff, 
    aes(y=reorder(Quarterback, Win_Differential), x=Rookie_Season_Wins, xend=Previous_Season_Wins),
    size = 2,
    colour = "grey",
    colour_x = "#800",
    colour_xend = "gold")+
  labs(
    title = "Jayden Daniels made an impact from day one",
    subtitle = "Daniels added eight wins to Washington's win total, second most in the pool. First place is a familiar face...",
    x = "Win Differential",
    y = "",
    caption="Source: Stathead Football | Ben Beecham"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 








There’s Dak Prescott again. He and Daniels are neck and neck for the biggest impact rookie quarterbacks ever. So what’s the verdict? Who is the best rookie quarterback ever?
Well, the answer is disappointing because it really just depends. Someone who values a quarterback’s dual-threat ability would say Daniels. Someone who thinks quarterbacks should stay in the pocket would probably say Prescott. The fact is, the quarterback position is incredibly nuanced and the main verdict is that most quarterback comparison discussions are stupid and pointless.

    subtitle = "The Spur has been able to make good use of his absurd eight-foot wingspan on the boards. He ranks seventh\namong first and second year players since 1990 in rebounds per 48 minutes, putting him right behind Shaq.",
    x = "",
    y = "Rebounds Per 48 Minutes",
    caption = "Source: Stathead Basketball | Ben Beecham"
  )+theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )









Before coming into the league, Wembanyama’s expectations were set so high that the only prospect many analysts compared him to was LeBron James. Although his offensive game is still far from The King’s, Wembanyama has separated himself as a one-of-a-kind combination of defensive prowess and offensive versatility. However, if he truly needs to meet his expectations, he needs to become more efficient on offense.
